top_stories.label: Top Stories
top_stories.ai_summary: It is a conflict-dominated evening. Israel says it has captured Lebanon's 12th-century Beaufort Castle and now controls roughly a fifth of the country, with Defence Minister Katz declaring a 'permanent presence' as Netanyahu orders troops deeper and new evacuation orders go out — Washington has effectively signalled support after its truce push collapsed. In Ukraine, the IAEA confirmed a drone struck a turbine building at the occupied Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant (radiation normal, Russia blames Kyiv), while overnight Russian strikes hit Odesa, Kharkiv and Chernihiv. US–Iran talks over the Strait of Hormuz remain unsigned, still awaiting Trump's sign-off.

stories.headline: Israel captures Beaufort Castle, declares 'permanent presence' as it occupies nearly a fifth of Lebanon
stories.summary: Israeli Defence Minister Israel Katz said troops had 'captured Beaufort once again and will remain there as part of the security zone in Lebanon,' planting a flag on the 12th-century fortress near Nabatieh in what he called 'a permanent presence.' Al Jazeera reports Israel now controls about 2,000 sq km — nearly one-fifth of Lebanon — after PM Netanyahu ordered forces to 'deepen and expand' the operation and Israel issued fresh evacuation orders for seven southern villages. Lebanon's health ministry says more than 3,412 people have been killed and 10,269 wounded since 2 March; Al Jazeera independently tallied at least 12 killed across the south on Sunday (Lebanese health-ministry figures).

stories.headline: US signals support for Israel's Lebanon push as US–Iran Hormuz deal stays unsigned
stories.summary: Bloomberg reports the US has effectively signalled support for Israel to expand operations in Lebanon after a fresh diplomatic initiative collapsed, with Secretary of State Marco Rubio having spoken to both Lebanese President Joseph Aoun and PM Netanyahu. In parallel, a tentative US–Iran memorandum that would extend the ceasefire 60 days and reopen the Strait of Hormuz still awaits President Trump's sign-off; Iran's foreign minister said 'it is not possible to judge' the outcome and disputes that the strait would leave Iranian control. Talks are being mediated by Pakistan.

stories.headline: Drone strikes turbine hall at occupied Zaporizhzhia nuclear plant; IAEA confirms damage
stories.summary: The IAEA said its on-site team confirmed damage to a turbine building at the Russian-occupied Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant consistent with a drone strike, including a damaged metal access hatch and burned optical-fibre debris; radiation levels remained normal and a follow-up inspection was interrupted by nearby drone activity and gunfire. Director-General Rafael Grossi said 'attacking nuclear sites is like playing with fire' — the first reported drone hit inside the plant perimeter since April 2024. Russia's Rosatom blamed a Ukrainian drone on Power Unit No. 6; Ukraine has not claimed the strike.

stories.headline: Overnight Russian strikes hit Odesa, Kharkiv and Chernihiv
stories.summary: Ukrainian officials said Russian strikes overnight into 1 June partially destroyed two floors of a building in Odesa and injured four, caused casualties in Kharkiv with garages and cars on fire, and injured four in the Dnipropetrovsk region; about 40,000 customers in Chernihiv were left without power. The barrage came as Ukraine's drone campaign against Russian oil infrastructure continued, with Kyiv saying its forces struck 18 Russian oil facilities during May (Ukrainian official figures).

singapore.label: Singapore
singapore.ai_summary: A quiet local evening centred on transport and weather. Tighter active-mobility rules took effect today: motorised personal mobility aids are now capped at 6km/h (down from 10km/h) and mobility-scooter users need a Certificate of Medical Need, with fines up to S$2,000 for breaches. NEA's 5.31pm update keeps it partly cloudy for the rest of tonight, with thundery showers over many areas before dawn tomorrow (25–34°C). In government news, Dr Koh Poh Koon's resignation as Senior Minister of State for Manpower and Health takes effect today; he remains an MP.

stories.headline: Tighter active-mobility rules take effect today: PMAs capped at 6km/h, medical certificate required
stories.summary: From 1 June, motorised Personal Mobility Aids must not exceed 6km/h (down from 10km/h) on public paths — about a brisk walking pace — and mobility-scooter users must hold a Certificate of Medical Need unless exempted (seniors aged 70+ and beneficiaries of selected disability/mobility schemes are auto-exempt). Existing devices have a transition period to 31 Dec 2028; breaches carry fines up to S$2,000 and/or up to six months' jail, per LTA.

stories.headline: NEA: Partly cloudy tonight, thundery showers returning before dawn tomorrow
stories.summary: NEA's 24-hour forecast updated at 5.31pm on 1 June expects it to stay partly cloudy for the rest of today, with thundery showers over many areas in the pre-dawn and early morning tomorrow. Temperatures of 25–34°C and southerly winds of 10–20km/h; NEA flags an imminent lightning risk where thundery showers occur.

stories.headline: Koh Poh Koon's resignation as Senior Minister of State takes effect today
stories.summary: Dr Koh Poh Koon relinquishes his appointments as Senior Minister of State for Manpower and for Health with effect from 1 June, after requesting on 22 May to step down from political office for family reasons; the Prime Minister accepted the resignation. Dr Koh, an MP for Tampines GRC, continues as a Member of Parliament after a decade as a political office holder.

wars.label: Wars & Conflicts
wars.ai_summary: Israel is the escalating actor in Lebanon: after seizing Beaufort Castle it declared a 'permanent presence' and now holds roughly a fifth of the country, with Netanyahu ordering deeper operations and fresh evacuation orders as Washington signals support and its truce push collapses. In Ukraine the danger this cycle is nuclear-safety: the IAEA confirmed a drone struck a turbine hall at the occupied Zaporizhzhia plant (radiation normal; Russia blames Kyiv, which has not claimed it), while Russian strikes hit Odesa, Kharkiv and Chernihiv overnight. US–Iran talks over reopening the Strait of Hormuz remain unsigned, still awaiting Trump's sign-off.

theatres.name: Russia–Ukraine War
theatres.status: Both sides trading strikes; drone hits Zaporizhzhia NPP turbine hall (radiation normal); Russian strikes on Ukrainian cities on 1 June

timeline.event: Russia launched one of its largest combined attacks of the war on Kyiv — Ukraine's Air Force said 90 missiles and 600 drones were used, including an Oreshnik hypersonic ballistic missile that hit Bila Tserkva. Zelensky reported 4 killed and almost 100 injured nationwide; air defences intercepted most drones and more than half the missiles (Ukrainian figures).

Ukrainian drones struck energy and industrial targets across multiple Russian regions overnight; Ukraine's military confirmed hitting the Saratov oil refinery and the Lazarevo oil-pumping station (Kirov region), with a separate strike on an oil facility in Armavir. Ukraine said its forces struck 18 Russian oil facilities during May (Ukrainian figures).

A drone struck a turbine building at the Russian-occupied Zaporizhzhia Nuclear Power Plant; the IAEA said the following day its on-site team confirmed damage consistent with a drone strike but that radiation levels remained normal, calling it the first reported drone hit inside the plant perimeter since April 2024. Russia's Rosatom blamed a Ukrainian drone on Power Unit No. 6; Ukraine has not claimed the strike (each side's account).

Ukrainian officials said overnight Russian strikes partially destroyed two floors of a building in Odesa (4 injured), caused casualties and fires in Kharkiv, injured four in the Dnipropetrovsk region, and left about 40,000 customers without power in Chernihiv (Ukrainian official figures).

theatres.name: Israel–Lebanon (Hezbollah)
theatres.status: Israel's deepest incursion in 25 years; controls ~one-fifth of Lebanon and declares a 'permanent presence' at Beaufort; ceasefire effectively collapsed

timeline.event: A US-brokered Israel–Lebanon ceasefire took effect; it was subsequently extended, but Hezbollah disarmament remained unresolved and both sides traded blame for violations.
Israel expanded its ground operation beyond the demarcation line it had set inside Lebanese territory after the 16 April ceasefire, as clashes intensified.

Netanyahu ordered troops to expand the ground manoeuvre; Israeli forces crossed the Litani River for the first time since 2006, seized the 12th-century Beaufort Castle near Nabatieh and ordered residents south of the Zahrani River (~14% of Lebanon) to evacuate. PM Salam accused Israel of a 'scorched-earth policy' (figures and characterisations attributed to the parties).

Defence Minister Israel Katz said troops had 'captured Beaufort once again and will remain there as part of the security zone,' planting a flag and calling it 'a permanent presence'; Al Jazeera reports Israel now controls about 2,000 sq km (~one-fifth of Lebanon), with Netanyahu ordering forces to 'deepen and expand' the operation and fresh evacuation orders for seven southern villages. Lebanon's health ministry put the toll since 2 March at more than 3,412 killed and 10,269 wounded; Al Jazeera tallied at least 12 killed across the south on Sunday. Bloomberg reported Washington has effectively signalled support after its truce push collapsed (Lebanese health-ministry figures; characterisations attributed to the parties).

theatres.name: US/Israel–Iran (Strait of Hormuz)
theatres.status: Ceasefire holding but unsigned; tentative MOU still awaiting Trump's sign-off, Iran disputes terms; talks mediated by Pakistan

timeline.event: Trump said a deal with Iran to reopen the Strait of Hormuz was 'largely negotiated' and would be announced soon; the framework would extend the ceasefire 60 days while the strait is de-mined and reopened. Iran's state-affiliated Fars disputed his characterization, claiming the strait would stay under Iranian control (Iranian state claim).

US and Iranian forces exchanged fire near the Strait of Hormuz: the US said it shot down four Iranian drones and struck a ground-control launch site at Bandar Abbas; Iran said it retaliated against a US airbase (each side's account). Trump said he was still 'not satisfied' with the draft.

US and Iran traded fresh drafts over the weekend seeking changes to an agreement that would extend the ceasefire 60 days and reopen Hormuz (including ending operations on all fronts and Iran disposing of its enriched-uranium stockpile); it was unclear whether the sides were making progress.

The tentative US–Iran memorandum reopening the Strait of Hormuz and extending the ceasefire 60 days remained unsigned, still awaiting President Trump's sign-off; Iran's foreign minister said 'it is not possible to judge' the outcome and Iran maintains the strait stays under its control. Talks are being mediated by Pakistan (each side's account).

theatres.name: Israel–Gaza
theatres.status: Fragile ceasefire under growing strain; near-daily Israeli strikes, severe aid shortfall

timeline.event: Human Rights Watch reported Israel curbed aid and killed civilians during the ceasefire; from 10 Oct 2025 to 27 May 2026 only about 49,973 of 135,600 allotted aid trucks (36%) entered Gaza, with truck flows collapsing after Israel closed crossings on 28 Feb.

Hamas confirmed an Israeli airstrike (26 May) killed Mohammed Odeh, the new head of its military wing in Gaza City, along with his wife and two children; Israel said Odeh was an 'architect' of the 7 Oct 2023 attack. Further strikes killed at least seven in Gaza City on 27 May, underscoring near-daily strikes during the 'ceasefire'.
Israel said it would not withdraw from the 'yellow line' inside Gaza; Netanyahu disclosed Israel had extended its control to about 60% of the Strip, with the enclave's long-term status unresolved.

outbreaks.label: Outbreaks
outbreaks.ai_summary: A high-mortality Bundibugyo Ebola epidemic in DRC and Uganda remains the dominant threat and is still escalating: 291 laboratory-confirmed cases and 43 confirmed deaths as of 31 May (US CDC, citing both health ministries: DRC 282/42, Uganda 9/1), well above WHO's 134/18 at a 29 May cutoff as suspected cases are reclassified. It has been a PHEIC since 17 May, with one exported case treated in Germany and no licensed Bundibugyo-specific vaccine. A small but deadly Andes hantavirus cluster from the MV Hondius cruise (13 cases, 3 deaths) is largely contained, cholera remains a vast multi-country burden, and US measles keeps climbing past 1,980 cases.

outbreaks.name: DRC/Uganda Bundibugyo Ebola (PHEIC)
outbreaks.status: Rapidly escalating; PHEIC since 17 May, no licensed Bundibugyo-specific vaccine/therapeutic
outbreaks.scope_note: Laboratory-confirmed cases/deaths only, DRC+Uganda combined (DRC 282 confirmed/42 deaths; Uganda 9 confirmed/1 death), per the US CDC Ebola Situation Summary as of 31 May (page last updated 31 May), citing both health ministries. The same metric in WHO Disease Outbreak News DON605 logged 134 confirmed/18 deaths at an earlier 29 May cutoff (DON605 remains the latest WHO DON); the gap reflects real DRC escalation plus reclassification of suspected cases into confirmed after lab results — NOT a confirmed-vs-suspected mismatch. DRC's separate suspected tally (906 suspected/223 suspected deaths per WHO as of 27 May) is NOT added to the confirmed figures above. One further confirmed case — a US doctor infected in DRC — is being treated in Germany and is not counted here.

outbreaks.name: MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster (multi-country)
outbreaks.status: Largely contained; cases among repatriated passengers, long incubation watched
outbreaks.scope_note: Total cases linked to the Dutch-flagged cruise MV Hondius: 11 confirmed + 2 probable = 13, with 3 deaths, per WHO Disease Outbreak News DON604 (as of 27 May), matching ECDC figures (26 May). Passengers/crew from 23 countries; cases and quarantine span 12+ countries (incl. Singapore, Canada, EU states). No US cases confirmed (CDC). Andes virus is the only hantavirus with documented person-to-person spread.

outbreaks.name: Multi-country cholera
outbreaks.status: Ongoing major burden; global risk assessed 'very high'
outbreaks.scope_note: Cumulative cholera + acute watery diarrhoea cases and deaths, 1 Jan–26 Apr 2026, across 22 countries in 4 WHO regions, per WHO Epi Update #37 (published 29 May; data cutoff 26 Apr, so figures lag ~5 weeks). Includes suspected + confirmed. Most-affected: Afghanistan (27,027), DRC (23,971), Mozambique (7,188), South Sudan (5,918), Yemen (4,073).

outbreaks.name: United States measles
outbreaks.scope_note: Confirmed US measles cases in 2026 across 40 jurisdictions, per CDC as of 28 May; 0 deaths reported in 2026. About 93% (1,847) outbreak-associated across 30 outbreaks; 92% of patients unvaccinated or of unknown status.

stories.headline: Bundibugyo Ebola outbreak past 290 confirmed cases in DRC and Uganda
stories.summary: As of 31 May the US CDC, citing the DRC and Uganda health ministries, reports 291 laboratory-confirmed Bundibugyo virus cases (DRC 282, Uganda 9) and 43 confirmed deaths — well above the 134 confirmed cases and 18 deaths in WHO's Disease Outbreak News DON605 at a 29 May cutoff. The rise reflects real escalation in DRC plus reclassification of suspected cases as confirmed, not a change in what is counted. The outbreak has been a PHEIC since 17 May, and there is no licensed vaccine or specific therapeutic for Bundibugyo virus.

stories.headline: US doctor with Bundibugyo Ebola evacuated to Germany for treatment
stories.summary: A medical doctor from the United States who was exposed while caring for patients in DRC tested positive for Bundibugyo virus on 17 May and was transported to Germany for care. The case is the outbreak's first documented export outside Africa and is counted separately from the confirmed cases in DRC and Uganda.

stories.headline: Andes hantavirus cruise-ship cluster reaches 13 cases, 3 deaths as repatriated passengers monitored
stories.summary: ECDC reports the MV Hondius Andes hantavirus cluster has grown to 13 cases (11 confirmed, 2 probable) with 3 deaths as of 26–27 May, with new cases emerging among repatriated passengers due to the virus's long incubation period. Passengers and crew came from 23 countries; monitoring and quarantine spanned 12-plus countries.

stories.headline: WHO: global cholera burden hits 79,740 cases and 1,010 deaths across 22 countries in 2026
stories.summary: WHO's Cholera Epidemiological Update #37, published 29 May, reports 79,740 cholera and acute watery diarrhoea cases and 1,010 deaths across 22 countries from 1 January to 26 April 2026, with global risk rated 'very high.' Afghanistan, DRC, Mozambique, South Sudan and Yemen are the most affected.

stories.headline: US measles cases pass 1,980 as outbreaks spread across 40 jurisdictions
stories.summary: CDC reported 1,983 confirmed measles cases for 2026 as of 28 May, across 40 jurisdictions, with no deaths so far this year. About 93% of cases are outbreak-associated across 30 outbreaks, and 92% of patients are unvaccinated or of unknown vaccination status.

cybersecurity.label: CyberSecurity
cybersecurity.ai_summary: Active exploitation dominates this cycle. A maximum-severity WordPress WP Maps Pro flaw (CVE-2026-8732) is seeing thousands of unauthenticated admin-takeover attempts, while the Palo Alto GlobalProtect auth-bypass (CVE-2026-0257) is now confirmed actively exploited and hit its CISA KEV remediation deadline today. A 19-year-old Linux CIFS privilege-escalation bug ('CIFSwitch') gives root across many distros, and both the FortiClient EMS flaw (CVE-2026-35616) and the LiteSpeed cPanel plugin bug (CVE-2026-48172) are being exploited to run code and drop infostealers. Two AI angles round out the week: attackers drove post-exploitation of a Marimo RCE with an LLM agent, and others abused ChatGPT share links to push malware.

stories.headline: WP Maps Pro bug exploited to create rogue admin accounts on WordPress sites
stories.summary: An unauthenticated access-control flaw in WP Maps Pro (<=6.1.0) lets attackers create administrator accounts and obtain a passwordless login URL, fully compromising the site. Wordfence reported blocking thousands of exploit attempts.

stories.headline: Palo Alto GlobalProtect VPN auth bypass flaw exploited; CISA mitigation deadline today
stories.summary: An authentication-bypass vulnerability in PAN-OS GlobalProtect portals/gateways lets attackers establish unauthorized VPN connections. It is now confirmed actively exploited (two attack waves observed from 17 May); Palo Alto confirmed limited attacks, and CISA — which added it to KEV — ordered federal agencies to mitigate it by 1 June.

stories.headline: New 'CIFSwitch' Linux flaw gives root on multiple distributions
stories.summary: A 19-year-old local privilege-escalation flaw in the Linux kernel CIFS subsystem lets unprivileged users forge cifs.spnego key requests to load malicious modules and gain root. A PoC is public and an upstream kernel patch is available; affected distros include Mint, CentOS Stream 9, Rocky/AlmaLinux 9, Kali and SUSE. No in-the-wild exploitation reported.

stories.headline: Attackers use LLM agent for post-exploitation after Marimo RCE exploit
stories.summary: After exploiting a critical pre-auth RCE in Marimo (<=0.20.4), an unknown actor used an LLM agent to drive post-compromise actions, harvesting cloud credentials and exfiltrating an internal PostgreSQL database in under an hour, per Sysdig research.

stories.headline: Charter Communications data breach affects 4.9 million accounts
stories.summary: Charter (Spectrum) confirmed a breach exposing 4.9 million accounts after a voice-phishing attack compromised an employee Microsoft Entra account and accessed Salesforce data. Exposed data included names, emails, phone numbers and addresses; the ShinyHunters group claimed responsibility.

stories.headline: Hackers abuse ChatGPT share links to push fake desktop-app malware
stories.summary: Threat actors are misusing ChatGPT's content-sharing feature to display fake OpenAI 'outage' pages that direct users to download malware disguised as the ChatGPT desktop application, per BleepingComputer.

stories.headline: Dutch police and NCSC disrupt 17-million-device Asocks proxy botnet
stories.summary: Dutch police and the national cyber agency (NCSC) seized roughly 200 command-and-control servers hosted in the Netherlands to disrupt the Asocks 'residential proxy' botnet, which had enrolled at least 17 million infected consumer devices (PCs, routers, phones and IoT) used to anonymise cyberattacks. Authorities warned the underlying devices remain infected.

stories.headline: Hackers exploit FortiClient EMS flaw to push infostealer malware
stories.summary: A pre-authentication API access-bypass flaw in FortiClient EMS (7.4.5/7.4.6) lets unauthenticated attackers escalate privileges and run commands. Threat actors are exploiting it to deliver the EKZ infostealer via fake Fortinet patch scripts; Fortinet fixed it in EMS 7.4.7 and CISA ordered federal remediation.

stories.headline: CISA warns LiteSpeed cPanel plugin flaw exploited to run scripts as root
stories.summary: A privilege-escalation flaw in the LiteSpeed cPanel/WHM plugin lets any cPanel user abuse the Redis enable/disable feature to execute scripts as root. CISA added it to KEV on 26 May with a short remediation deadline after observing active exploitation.
